statement.title: Infinite discrete definable sets in an o-minimal line
statement.body: Let M be an o-minimal expansion of a real closed field and let A subseteq M be definable with parameters. A colleague claims that A can be a countably infinite discrete subset of M with no accumulation point in M. Decide whether this is possible and justify your answer from the defining structure of one-variable definable sets in o-minimality.
notation_conventions: subseteq is ordinary set inclusion in M
discrete means every point of A is isolated in the subspace topology

goal.success_criteria: The answer should conclude that the claim is impossible and explain that a definable subset of the line is a finite union of points and intervals, ruling out an infinite discrete definable subset.

source.citation: Original benchmark item inspired by standard o-minimality facts.
source.provenance_notes: Commissioned for the benchmark as a research-literacy item.

common_failure_modes: asserts countable sets are allowed because countable unions of points are definable
mentions cell decomposition without connecting it to the conclusion

statement.title: Triangle-free does not force bounded chromatic number
statement.body: Give a mathematically valid explanation that the property of being triangle-free does not bound chromatic number uniformly. Your answer may use an explicit construction family or a standard existence argument, but it should make clear what phenomenon is being demonstrated.
notation_conventions: chromatic number means ordinary vertex chromatic number
prerequisites_assumed: familiarity with high-chromatic constructions or probabilistic existence methods

goal.success_criteria: The answer should provide or cite a genuine family of triangle-free graphs with arbitrarily large chromatic number, such as Mycielski constructions or Erdos-type existence, and explain the conceptual consequence.
forbidden_shortcuts: claiming the fact without naming any valid construction or existence method

source.citation: Original benchmark item inspired by standard extremal graph theory examples.
source.provenance_notes: Designed as a hypothesis-sensitivity item for combinatorics.

common_failure_modes: confuses large girth with triangle-free
gives a finite example with high but bounded chromatic number without addressing unboundedness

statement.title: Finitely generated torsion linear groups are finite
statement.body: Suppose G is a finitely generated subgroup of GL_n(C) and every element of G has finite order. Decide whether G must be finite and justify your answer by invoking an appropriate structural theorem or argument.
notation_conventions: GL_n(C) is the group of invertible complex n by n matrices
prerequisites_assumed: standard structural results for linear groups

goal.success_criteria: The answer should conclude that G is finite and justify this using a valid theorem such as Schur's theorem on finitely generated torsion linear groups or an equivalent argument.
forbidden_shortcuts: asserting Burnside without enough context to support the conclusion

source.citation: Original benchmark item built from a standard structural algebra fact.

common_failure_modes: confuses torsion with finite exponent without justification
states Lie-Kolchin, which is irrelevant here

statement.title: Local points on a genus-zero curve over a number field
statement.body: Let C be a smooth projective curve of genus 0 over a number field K. Assume C has a K_v-rational point for every completion K_v of K. Explain why one may conclude that C(K) is nonempty.
notation_conventions: K_v denotes a completion of K at a place v

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that a smooth projective genus-zero curve is a conic after choosing an embedding and that the Hasse-Minkowski principle for conics yields a K-rational point.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating the Hasse principle without connecting genus zero to conics

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on standard local-global theory for conics.
source.provenance_notes: Designed as a research-literacy arithmetic geometry question.

common_failure_modes: forgets projective smooth genus-zero curves need not have a K-point a priori
quotes Riemann-Roch without addressing local data

statement.title: Maximal Cohen-Macaulay modules over a regular local ring
statement.body: Let R be a regular local ring and M a finitely generated R-module with depth_R(M)=dim(R). Explain why M must be free. A complete proof is not required, but the argument should identify the structural theorem that closes the reasoning.
notation_conventions: depth_R(M) is the module depth over R

goal.success_criteria: The answer should identify M as maximal Cohen-Macaulay, use that regular local rings have finite global dimension, deduce pd_R(M) is finite, and then apply Auslander-Buchsbaum to obtain pd_R(M)=0 and hence freeness.
forbidden_shortcuts: claiming all maximal Cohen-Macaulay modules are free without using regularity

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on standard commutative algebra reasoning.
source.provenance_notes: Designed to probe theorem chaining rather than brute-force computation.

common_failure_modes: uses Cohen-Macaulay of R but not regularity
omits the finite projective dimension input needed for Auslander-Buchsbaum

statement.title: Higher Ext over a hereditary finite-dimensional algebra
statement.body: Let A be a finite-dimensional hereditary algebra over a field. Explain why Ext_A^2(X,Y)=0 for all finite-dimensional A-modules X and Y.
notation_conventions: Ext_A^i denotes Yoneda Ext in the module category

goal.success_criteria: The answer should state that hereditary means global dimension at most 1, so all modules admit projective resolutions of length at most 1, forcing Ext^2 to vanish.

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard finite-dimensional algebra theory.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a clean representation-theoretic calibration item.

common_failure_modes: confuses hereditary with every submodule of a projective being injective
omits the resolution argument

statement.title: Birational morphisms of normal projective curves
statement.body: Let f:X -> Y be a birational morphism of projective curves over an algebraically closed field. Assume X is smooth and Y is normal. Explain why f must be an isomorphism.
notation_conventions: normal for curves means nonsingular in codimension one, hence regular

goal.success_criteria: The answer should observe that a normal projective curve is smooth, identify f as a finite birational morphism between smooth projective curves, and conclude by the universal property of normalization or degree considerations that f is an isomorphism.
forbidden_shortcuts: saying birational maps between projective varieties are always isomorphisms

source.citation: Original benchmark item inspired by standard facts on curves and normalization.
source.provenance_notes: Serves as a research-literacy birational geometry prompt.

ambiguity_risks: the item relies on standard facts about normal curves
common_failure_modes: forgets to use normality of Y
confuses rational maps with morphisms

statement.title: Degree one self-map of the sphere
statement.body: Let f:S^n -> S^n be a continuous map of degree 1. Explain why f cannot be null-homotopic.
notation_conventions: degree means the induced map on top homology

goal.success_criteria: The answer should note that a null-homotopic map induces the zero map on reduced homology, whereas a degree-one map induces the identity on H_n(S^n), so the map cannot be null-homotopic.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating 'degree is a homotopy invariant' without tying it to null-homotopy

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard algebraic topology.
source.provenance_notes: Used as a topology baseline with proof-style justification.

common_failure_modes: conflates degree one with homeomorphism
argues by fundamental group even when n>1

statement.title: Exactness on a closed symplectic manifold
statement.body: Let (M,omega) be a compact symplectic manifold without boundary. Can omega be exact? Give a justification that makes clear which geometric hypothesis is doing the work.
notation_conventions: closed means compact and without boundary

goal.success_criteria: The answer should conclude that omega cannot be exact on a closed symplectic manifold because if omega=dalpha, then integral of omega^n equals integral of d(alpha wedge omega^(n-1)), which vanishes by Stokes, contradicting positivity of symplectic volume.
forbidden_shortcuts: asserting nonexactness without using compactness and no-boundary

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on a standard symplectic obstruction.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a geometry hypothesis-sensitivity question.

common_failure_modes: forgets compactness or boundarylessness
states volume positivity without relating it to Stokes

statement.title: Hartogs extension across a compact set
statement.body: Let K be a compact subset of C^n with n at least 2, and assume C^n minus K is connected. Explain why every holomorphic function on C^n \ K extends holomorphically to all of C^n.
notation_conventions: C^n \ K denotes the complement of K

goal.success_criteria: The answer should identify Hartogs extension as the relevant theorem and explain that in several complex variables, unlike one complex variable, isolated compact holes in a connected complement do not obstruct global extension.
forbidden_shortcuts: claiming removable singularity theorem from one variable without addressing the higher-dimensional phenomenon

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on the classical Hartogs extension theorem.
source.provenance_notes: Serves as an advanced analysis calibration item.

common_failure_modes: confuses the one-variable and several-variable situations
omits the connected complement condition from the explanation

statement.title: Spectrum of a self-adjoint element in a C-star algebra
statement.body: Let a be a self-adjoint element of a unital C-star algebra. Explain why the spectrum of a is contained in the real line.

goal.success_criteria: The answer should show that if lambda has nonzero imaginary part then (a-lambda 1)^*(a-lambda 1) is invertible, hence a-lambda 1 is invertible, so lambda is not in the spectrum.
forbidden_shortcuts: asserting the spectral theorem for Hilbert space operators without adapting it to general C-star algebras

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard operator algebra theory.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a concise but nontrivial operator-theoretic item.

common_failure_modes: uses Hilbert-space spectral theorem too narrowly
does not explain invertibility for nonreal lambda

statement.title: Minimality versus mixing for irrational circle rotations
statement.body: Consider the rotation T_alpha(x)=x+alpha mod 1 on the circle, where alpha is irrational. Explain why T_alpha is minimal but not mixing. The answer should distinguish clearly between the two dynamical notions rather than only naming them.
notation_conventions: minimal means every orbit is dense
mixing means measure-theoretic mixing with respect to Lebesgue measure

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that irrational rotations have dense orbits, giving minimality, and that they have pure point spectrum / rigid correlation behavior preventing mixing.
forbidden_shortcuts: asserting 'rotations are isometries so not mixing' without mathematical explanation

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on a standard contrast in dynamics.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a comparison-of-frameworks dynamics question.

common_failure_modes: confuses transitivity with minimality
asserts nonmixing without discussing eigenfunctions or correlation obstruction
